edges.description: Diminished fetal movement produces congenital webbing or pterygia.
The fetal hypokinesia sequence places the disorder in the arthrogryposis multiplex congenita spectrum.
Reduced prenatal movement produces congenital joint contractures.
Decreased fetal movement is the observable prenatal expression of fetal hypokinesia.
Oligohydramnios can accompany the prenatal fetal hypokinesia presentation.
Diminished fetal movement contributes to characteristic craniofacial deformation including micrognathia.
intermediate_mechanisms: Reduced prenatal jaw movement alters craniofacial growth.

edges.description: The fetal hypokinesia sequence includes characteristic facial features such as downslanted palpebral fissures.
Ptosis is a characteristic facial manifestation of the diminished-fetal-movement sequence.
Palate abnormalities are craniofacial findings in the same congenital deformation sequence.
Low-set ears are a characteristic facial manifestation of the diminished-fetal-movement sequence.

edges.source: Non-fusion spinal instrumentation for severe scoliosis

nodes.description: Multiple congenital contractures across body regions place the disorder in the arthrogryposis multiplex congenita spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Multiple pterygium syndrome (MPS) is a genetically heterogeneous rare form of arthrogryposis multiplex congenita characterized by joint contractures and webbing or pterygia, as well as distinctive facial features related to diminished fetal movement.

evidence.reference_title: CHRNG-related nonlethal multiple pterygium syndrome: Muscle imaging pattern and clinical, histopathological, and molecular genetic findings.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the CHRNG gene cause autosomal recessive multiple pterygium syndrome (MPS).

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Pathogenic recessive variants in CHRNG were found in five patients, all of whom had compound heterozygous variants (Table 4).

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Among the five different CHRNG variants found in these individuals, four led to premature termination codons and one represented an in-frame duplication (p.Trp98_Leu100dup) that had been described before [11].

nodes.description: Conservative spine management with bracing and serial casting can be used for early scoliosis before escalation to growth-friendly instrumentation in more severe curves.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Bracing and serial spine casting appear to be beneficial for a few years; non-fusion spinal instrumentation may be needed to modulate more severe curves during growth and spontaneous spine fusions may occur in those cases.

nodes.description: Reduced fetal movement is a prenatal clue and a mechanistic driver of the congenital contracture and pterygium sequence.

evidence.reference_title: Nonlethal multiple pterygium syndrome: Escobar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: A case of nonlethal neonatal Escobar is reported in a 35-week-and-6-day old infant who presented in utero with decreased fetal movement, oligohydramnios, and arthrogryposis.

nodes.description: Downslanted palpebral fissures are a common facial feature in the Escobar-type MPS case series.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Downslanting palpebral fissures were observed in 10 patients (Table 2).

nodes.description: Pathogenic CHRNG variants impair the fetal gamma subunit of the muscle nicotinic acetylcholine receptor. Because this receptor subunit is required during embryonic and fetal neuromuscular junction development, disruption reduces fetal neuromuscular transmission before the adult receptor subunit replaces it later in gestation.

evidence.reference_title: Truncating CHRNG mutations associated with interfamilial variability of the severity of the Escobar variant of multiple pterygium syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Mutations in the CHRNG encoding the embryonal acetylcholine receptor may cause the non-lethal Escobar variant (EVMPS) and lethal form (LMPS) of multiple pterygium syndrome.

evidence.reference_title: A Truncating Variant of CHRNG as a Cause of Escobar Syndrome: A Multiple Pterygium Syndrome Subtype.
evidence.snippet: It is most frequently due to a genetic variant in CHRNG , which encodes the γ-subunit of the nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.

nodes.description: Reduced fetal neuromuscular signaling decreases fetal movement. The resulting fetal hypokinesia produces secondary deformation features, including congenital pterygia, arthrogryposis, scoliosis, and craniofacial findings related to diminished movement.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Multiple pterygium syndrome (MPS) is a genetically heterogeneous rare form of arthrogryposis multiplex congenita characterized by joint contractures and webbing or pterygia, as well as distinctive facial features related to diminished fetal movement.

nodes.description: Fixed congenital limitation of joint motion is common and may involve limbs, digits, shoulders, elbows, hips, knees, ankles, or other joints.

evidence.reference_title: Nonlethal multiple pterygium syndrome: Escobar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: It is a form of arthrogryposis multiplex congenita characterized by excessive webbing (pterygia), congenital contractures (arthrogryposis), and scoliosis.

nodes.description: Low-set or posteriorly rotated ears are recurrent facial features in the pediatric MPS case series.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Four patients had both posterior rotated ears and low-set ears, whereas another three patients either had posterior rotated ears or low-set ears.

nodes.description: Small mandible is a characteristic craniofacial feature related to diminished fetal movement.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Other characteristic findings of MPS are short stature, webbing of the neck, and distinctive facial features including micrognathia, cleft palate, down-turned corners of the mouth, an elongated philtrum, down-slanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, ptosis, and low-set ears, all of which are related to diminished fetal movement.

nodes.description: Severe early-onset scoliosis may require growth-friendly non-fusion spinal instrumentation after careful spine monitoring and conservative measures.
nodes.id: Non-fusion spinal instrumentation for severe scoliosis

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Bracing and serial spine casting appear to be beneficial for a few years; non-fusion spinal instrumentation may be needed to modulate more severe curves during growth and spontaneous spine fusions may occur in those cases.

nodes.description: Reduced amniotic fluid can accompany the fetal hypokinesia presentation in prenatal or neonatal Escobar syndrome cases.

evidence.reference_title: Nonlethal multiple pterygium syndrome: Escobar syndrome.
evidence.snippet: A case of nonlethal neonatal Escobar is reported in a 35-week-and-6-day old infant who presented in utero with decreased fetal movement, oligohydramnios, and arthrogryposis.

nodes.description: Cleft palate or high palate is a recurrent craniofacial finding in the Escobar-type MPS series.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Three patients had cleft palate and another five had high palate.

nodes.description: Congenital webbing occurs across multiple flexural body regions and is a defining feature of the disorder.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Characteristic phenotypic features of Escobar were observed in all 12 patients, including webbing over the neck, axillary, elbow, knee and/or fingers (Table 1).

nodes.description: Eyelid ptosis is one of the characteristic facial findings related to diminished fetal movement in MPS.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Other characteristic findings of MPS are short stature, webbing of the neck, and distinctive facial features including micrognathia, cleft palate, down-turned corners of the mouth, an elongated philtrum, down-slanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, ptosis, and low-set ears, all of which are related to diminished fetal movement.

nodes.description: Developmental spinal curvature is common, may begin early, and can require bracing, casting, or growth-friendly instrumentation in severe cases.

evidence.reference_title: The Clinical and Genotypic Spectrum of Scoliosis in Multiple Pterygium Syndrome: A Case Series on 12 Children.
evidence.snippet: Scoliosis was present in all but our youngest patient.
